response:
Summary (root cause)
- We found the retry loop was caused by the service token containing special XML characters (eg. <, &, etc.) that broke when injected directly into the APIM policy XML. That corruption made APIM treat the header/policy value as invalid and the policy retried the token exchange/path repeatedly.

Immediate debugging checklist (use this to reproduce and confirm)
1. Confirm the symptom
   - Reproduce a failing call and capture the APIM trace (enable “Trace” in the APIM developer portal or use the APIM diagnostics logs).
   - Look for repeated attempts (retries) of the send-request / token-exchange or repeated backend calls in the trace.

2. Check DDI tenant isolation and tri-auth pieces (per established pattern)
   - Ensure x-auth-ddi header is present and correct (missing/misconfigured x-auth-ddi is a common failure point).
   - Ensure Azure AD app registration and scopes used for the token exchange are correct.
   - Confirm subscription-key (if used) is present and valid.

3. Inspect the APIM policy XML where the token is injected
   - Is the token being placed directly as literal text inside an XML element in the policy? (This is the likely culprit.)
   - Is the token taken from a named value or inserted via a policy expression/variable?

Why it breaks
- Putting raw token text containing XML-significant characters directly into the policy XML will break the policy parser or create malformed headers. That leads to failures and the retry logic in the policy will keep attempting the exchange.

Fixes (recommended)
1. Do not inline the raw token into the XML policy. Use policy expressions / variables to set header values at runtime.
   - Example pattern:
     - Use send-request to obtain the token (or whichever call gets it).
     - Store the token in a variable with <set-variable>.
     - Use <set-header> with an expression to set the header value: the expression inserts the token at runtime and avoids placing raw special characters into the policy source.
   - Example (conceptual):
     <send-request ...> ... </send-request>
     <set-variable name="serviceToken" value="@(context.Response.Body.As<JObject>()["access_token"].ToString())" />
     <set-header name="Authorization" exists-action="override">@("Bearer " + context.Variables["serviceToken"])</set-header>

2. If you must include the token text in XML for some reason, escape the XML special characters (e.g. & -> &, < -> <, > -> >). Better to avoid this entirely and use expressions/variables.

3. If token consumer requires raw characters, ensure APIM sets the header value (as above) so it arrives unescaped on the outbound HTTP request. APIM will put the evaluated string into the HTTP header; the XML escaping only matters to the policy file itself.

4. If token is created/returned by an upstream call and contains problematic characters, consider:
   - Changing the upstream token generator to avoid those characters (preferred if possible).
   - Or safe-encoding the token in the exchange: e.g., return it base64/url-encoded and decode at the consumer (only if both ends can be changed).

Verification after fix
- Re-run failing scenario and check APIM trace:
  - The policy parsing errors should be gone.
  - Token exchange should succeed and no repeated retries should occur.
- Check actual outbound request headers to backend to confirm Authorization (or other header) contains the correct token.
- Confirm x-auth-ddi header remains present and correct.

Other operational notes
- Enable APIM tracing and Application Insights to capture the policy execution steps during testing.
- Named values: if you store tokens in APIM named values, ensure you are not inserting them as raw XML literals in policies.
- If gateway misconfiguration caused x-auth-ddi to be dropped previously, validate gateway routes and policies that may strip headers.

If you want, I can:
- Review the exact failing APIM policy XML and point to the exact line to change.
- Provide a full concrete policy snippet (token fetch -> store -> set-header) based on your token response shape.
